steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses: (1) “…the Emmy, Grammy, Oscar, and Tony [blank]” and (2) “the achievement is known as ‘winning the EGOT.’” A semicolon is the correct punctuation to join two independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction. The word respectively modifies the list of awards in the first clause, so it must stay with that clause before the semicolon.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘awards, respectively,’: Two commas around respectively treat what follows as a continuation of the first clause — but “the achievement is known as…” is an independent clause. A comma alone cannot join two independent clauses (comma splice). ✗
  • Choice B — ‘awards; respectively’: A semicolon must be followed by a space, then the start of an independent clause — not a single adverb. “respectively” is not an independent clause and cannot directly follow a semicolon here. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘awards, respectively—’: A dash at the end would introduce supplementary information or an interruption, but “the achievement is known as…” is a full independent clause and needs a stronger separator than a dash used this way. This creates a structural mismatch with the earlier em dash in the sentence. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘awards, respectively;’: Respectively wraps up the first clause (the list of awards), and the semicolon correctly joins it to the second independent clause. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
